speaker: doctor
text: Good morning. Let's start with your vitals from this morning — your body temperature is 36.2 °C, which is within the normal range.

speaker: doctor
text: Your hemoglobin level is 129 g/L — that’s slightly low for an adult male but still in the acceptable range; we’ll keep an eye on it.

speaker: doctor
text: Your diastolic blood pressure today is 95 mmHg, which is elevated — we’ll want to monitor that closely, especially given your diabetes history.

speaker: doctor
text: Your fasting blood glucose is 6.6 mmol/L — just above target for optimal control. How has your home monitoring been going?

speaker: patient
text: It’s been up and down. Some mornings it’s around 6.2, other days closer to 7.4 — I’ve been trying to stick to my meal plan.

speaker: doctor
text: That’s helpful context. Any new symptoms lately — fatigue, thirst, frequent urination, or changes in vision?

speaker: patient
text: Yes — I’ve noticed blurred vision over the past two days, especially when reading or driving at night.

speaker: doctor
text: Blurred vision can be a sign of fluctuating glucose levels — let’s correlate that with your recent readings. Was your glucose higher on the days you noticed the blurring?

speaker: doctor
text: Given your current pattern and the new symptom, I’m prescribing Insulin glargine 10 units once daily at bedtime to improve overnight and fasting control.

speaker: patient
text: Okay — I’ll start tonight. Should I adjust my current rapid-acting insulin dose too?

speaker: doctor
text: Not yet — we’ll hold off for now and reassess in one week. Please log your fasting glucose, pre-meal, and bedtime values, plus any episodes of blurred vision.

speaker: patient
text: Will do. Also, I checked my blood sugar this morning before breakfast — it was 6.8 mmol/L, so a bit higher than the 6.6 you recorded earlier.

speaker: doctor
text: Thanks for that — that reading is consistent with what we saw in clinic, though slightly elevated. Let’s see how it trends with the new basal insulin.

speaker: patient
text: One more thing — last week my diastolic was 88 mmHg, but today it’s 95. Is that something to worry about?

speaker: doctor
text: Yes, that rise is meaningful — the 95 mmHg we measured today is higher than the 88 you mentioned from last week. We’ll want to rule out white-coat effect and consider lifestyle adjustments or possible antihypertensive initiation if it persists.

speaker: patient
text: Makes sense. And my hemoglobin — you said it was 129. That’s actually lower than the 134 from my lab last month. Any concern there?

speaker: doctor
text: Good catch — yes, that drop from 134 to 129 is modest but worth tracking. We’ll repeat it in four weeks and check ferritin and B12 to assess for early iron or vitamin deficiency.

speaker: patient
text: Got it. Also, I’ve been using the same glucometer for six months — could calibration drift explain some of the variation?

speaker: doctor
text: Possibly — let’s verify today’s reading with our in-office meter to confirm accuracy. If there’s a discrepancy, we’ll replace your test strips and review technique.

speaker: patient
text: That’d be great — thanks. I’ll bring my logbook and meter next time.
